Name: v3: NakedClaude Demo
Description: Rulebook generated from Airtable base 'v3: NakedClaude Demo'.

Customers.summary_rich: The **cast** of v3. Each customer has a current `Status` (linked, not free-text) and a stream of `Orders`. The `IsStopped` flag composes a *blocking status* with the customer's order history, so the same customer can be flagged for two independent reasons — and the rulebook records *which* reason without any branching in app code.

schema.name: Name
schema.Description: Human-readable customer name.

schema.name: Notes
schema.Description: Free-form notes about the customer.

schema.name: IsStopped
schema.Description: True when the customer's current Status is a blocking status. Lookup of Statuses.IsBlocking.

schema.name: StatusIsBlocking
schema.Description: True when the customer's current Status is a blocking status. Lookup of Statuses.IsBlocking.

schema.name: Status
schema.Description: Link to the customer's current Status in the Statuses table.

schema.name: StatusDisplayName
schema.Description: Human-readable display name of the customer's current Status. Lookup of Statuses.DisplayName.

schema.name: StatusDescription
schema.Description: Description of the customer's current Status. Lookup of Statuses.Description.

schema.name: Orders
schema.Description: Reverse link to all Orders placed by this customer.

schema.name: Email
schema.Description: Primary email address for the customer.

schema.name: Phone
schema.Description: Primary phone number for the customer.

schema.name: CompanyName
schema.Description: Company the customer represents, if applicable.

schema.name: BillingAddress
schema.Description: Default billing address for the customer.

schema.name: ShippingAddress
schema.Description: Default shipping address for the customer.

schema.name: CreatedAt
schema.Description: Timestamp when the customer record was created.

schema.name: HasMultiPaymentOrders
schema.explanation_rich: **Why a generic formula isn't enough — and how v3 fixes it.** Naively, `HasMultiPaymentOrders` is *true when at least one of this customer's orders has more than one payment*. The generic transpiler approximates this with `CountOfMultiPaymentOrders > 0`, which is wrong: the underlying COUNTIFS counts *all* of a customer's orders, not orders with multiple payments. v3's fix lives in `ERBCustomizations` as `calc_customers_has_multi_payment_orders` — a hand-written Postgres function that asks the right question (`EXISTS … WHERE COUNT(payments) > 1`). Worked example: Caroline has one order (`caroline-1045`) with two payments, so the SQL override returns TRUE. Bobby has two orders, each with one payment, so the override returns FALSE — exactly the opposite of what the naive `COUNTIFS > 0` would say.

Orders.summary_rich: The **heart of v3**. Every order rolls up its line items into `SubTotal`, adds tax to make `OrderTotal`, then watches `Payments` aggregate into `TotalPaid` so `AmountDue` and `PaymentStatusLabel` self-correct in real time. This is where the formula DAG stops being a toy — multi-payment, partial-payment, and refunded states all resolve correctly without a single line of imperative code.

schema.name: Name
schema.Description: Lowercase compound key combining the linked Customer and OrderNumber (e.g. 'bob-1001'). Used as the natural key for the order.

schema.name: OrderNumber
schema.Description: Sequential or human-assigned order identifier (e.g. '1001'). Combined with Customer to form the order's natural key.

schema.name: Customer
schema.Description: Link to the Customer who placed this order.

schema.name: OrderLineItems
schema.Description: Reverse link to all OrderLineItems on this order.

schema.name: Payments
schema.Description: Reverse link to all Payments applied to this order.

schema.name: OrderDate
schema.Description: When the order was placed.

schema.name: OrderStatus
schema.Description: Lifecycle state of the order.

schema.name: ShippingAddress
schema.Description: Where this specific order ships to.

schema.name: BillingAddress
schema.Description: Billing address used for this specific order.

schema.name: Notes
schema.Description: Free-form notes about this order.

schema.name: TaxRate
schema.Description: Sales tax rate applied to this order.

schema.name: AmountDue
schema.explanation_rich: **The cash register's bottom line.** `AmountDue = ROUND(OrderTotal - TotalPaid, 2)`. `OrderTotal` is itself a calc (`SubTotal + TaxAmount`) and `TotalPaid` is an aggregation over *completed* payments only (via `Payments.CompletedAmount`, which is zero for Pending/Failed/Refunded). Worked example: Caroline's `caroline-1045` totals $126.82. Her first payment (`pmt-1`) was *Failed* — `CompletedAmount = 0`. Her second (`pmt-2`) was *Completed* for $126.82. `TotalPaid = $126.82`, so `AmountDue = ROUND(126.82 - 126.82, 2) = $0.00`. Reverse the second payment's status to `Failed` and the same field flips to `$126.82` on the next read.

schema.name: IsPaidInFull
schema.explanation_rich: **Boolean derived from the bottom line.** `IsPaidInFull = (AmountDue <= 0)`. The `<=` (not `=`) catches overpayment and rounding-noise cases where `AmountDue` lands at a tiny negative like `-0.0001`. Worked example: Alice's order `alice-johnson-1010` totals $4,770.44; two completed payments ($1,500 + $3,270.44) sum to $4,770.44; `AmountDue = 0`, so `IsPaidInFull = TRUE`. Caroline's `caroline-1045` is also TRUE (despite the failed first attempt) because only completed payments count in `TotalPaid`.

schema.name: PaymentStatusLabel
schema.explanation_rich: **The human-readable verdict.** Three-way label: `IF(IsPaidInFull, 'Paid', IF(TotalPaid = 0, 'Unpaid', 'Partial'))`. The ordering matters — `IsPaidInFull` is checked *first* because a fully-paid order can still have multiple non-zero `TotalPaid` contributions. Worked example: Carla's refunded order `carla-smith-1024` had a payment row, but `CompletedAmount = 0` because the payment was refunded — so `TotalPaid = 0`, `IsPaidInFull = FALSE`, and the label resolves to *Unpaid*. A partial payment ($50 of $200) would instead resolve to *Partial*.

data.SQLCode:
-- FunctionOverride: calc_customers_average_order_value
-- Errors in this file are non-fatal (runs without ON_ERROR_STOP).

CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION public.calc_customers_average_order_value(p_customer_id text)
 RETURNS TEXT
 LANGUAGE plpgsql
 STABLE SECURITY DEFINER
AS $function$
DECLARE
  v_avg numeric;
BEGIN
  SELECT AVG(calc_orders_order_total(order_id))
    INTO v_avg
    FROM orders
   WHERE customer = p_customer_id;
  RETURN COALESCE(ROUND(v_avg, 2)::text, '0.00');
END;
$function$;
data.Name: calc_customers_average_order_value

data.SQLCode:
-- FunctionOverride: calc_customers_has_multi_payment_orders
-- Errors in this file are non-fatal (runs without ON_ERROR_STOP).

CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION public.calc_customers_has_multi_payment_orders(p_customer_id text)
 RETURNS BOOLEAN
 LANGUAGE plpgsql
 STABLE SECURITY DEFINER
AS $function$
BEGIN
  RETURN EXISTS (
    SELECT 1
      FROM orders o
     WHERE o.customer = p_customer_id
       AND (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM payments p WHERE p."order" = o.order_id) > 1
  );
END;
$function$;
data.Title: Fix: only true when customer has an order with >1 payment
data.Name: calc_customers_has_multi_payment_orders
calc_customers_days_since_last_order

data.SQLCode:
-- FunctionOverride: calc_orders_item_count
-- Errors in this file are non-fatal (runs without ON_ERROR_STOP).

CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION public.calc_orders_item_count(p_order_id text)
 RETURNS integer
 LANGUAGE plpgsql
 STABLE SECURITY DEFINER
AS $function$
BEGIN
  RETURN ((SELECT COUNT(*) FROM order_line_items WHERE "order" = p_order_id))::integer;
END;
$function$;
data.Name: calc_orders_item_count

data.SQLCode:
-- FunctionOverride: calc_orders_last_payment_date
-- Errors in this file are non-fatal (runs without ON_ERROR_STOP).

CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION public.calc_orders_last_payment_date(p_order_id text)
 RETURNS timestamp with time zone
 LANGUAGE plpgsql
 STABLE SECURITY DEFINER
AS $function$
BEGIN
  RETURN (SELECT MAX(payment_date) FROM payments WHERE "order" = p_order_id);
END;
$function$;
data.Name: calc_orders_last_payment_date

data.SQLCode:
-- FunctionOverride: calc_orders_payment_count
-- Errors in this file are non-fatal (runs without ON_ERROR_STOP).

CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION public.calc_orders_payment_count(p_order_id text)
 RETURNS integer
 LANGUAGE plpgsql
 STABLE SECURITY DEFINER
AS $function$
BEGIN
  RETURN ((SELECT COUNT(*) FROM payments WHERE "order" = p_order_id))::integer;
END;
$function$;
data.Name: calc_orders_payment_count

__meta__.Description: Project-level metadata that travels with the rulebook: tagline, motif, narrative descriptions, substrate list, signature rows, etc. One row per metadata key. Use ValueType to interpret StringValue vs JsonValue.

schema.name: MetaKey
schema.Description: The metadata key (e.g. 'tagline', 'motif_palette', 'substrates'). Unique within the table.

schema.name: Name
schema.Description: Identifier for this metadata entry. Mirrors MetaKey so the row is addressable by Name like every other table.

schema.name: ValueType
schema.Description: How to interpret the value columns: 'string' (use StringValue), 'object' (parse JsonValue as JSON object), 'array' (parse JsonValue as JSON array).

schema.name: StringValue
schema.Description: Plain string value. Populated when ValueType == 'string'; null otherwise.

schema.name: JsonValue
schema.Description: JSON-encoded value. Populated when ValueType == 'object' or 'array'; null when ValueType == 'string'.

data.Name: tagline
data.StringValue: Order-to-cash, end-to-end — with the first real SQL escape hatches.

data.Name: description_rich
data.StringValue: v3 is the rung where NakedClaude grows up. v2 modeled three tables (customers, statuses, app users) and stopped at *can this customer place an order?* — v3 lets them actually **place one, pay for it, and have the books balance**. Eight tables (`Customers`, `Statuses`, `Products`, `Orders`, `OrderLineItems`, `Payments`, plus `ERBVersions` and `ERBCustomizations`) wire an entire order-to-cash pipeline as a DAG of calculated fields: line-item subtotals roll into order subtotals, tax lands on top, payments aggregate into `TotalPaid`, and `AmountDue` / `IsPaidInFull` / `PaymentStatusLabel` fall out automatically. The new-at-this-rung capability is `ERBCustomizations` — hand-written Postgres function overrides for the handful of calculations the generic formula transpiler can't express cleanly (`AverageOrderValue`, `DaysSinceLastOrder`, `HasMultiPaymentOrders`). This is the first rung that demonstrates the *escape hatch* without breaking the substrate-equivalence claim: the override is named, versioned, and shipped inside the rulebook itself.

data.Name: use_cases
data.JsonValue: ["**Reconcile a multi-payment order.** Open Caroline's order `caroline-1045` ($126.82). One payment failed, the second succeeded — watch `TotalPaid` aggregate only completed payments, `AmountDue` go to zero, and `PaymentStatusLabel` flip to *Paid*.", "**Refund a returned order.** Carla's `carla-smith-1024` was refunded; `CompletedAmount` drops the refunded payment out of the rollup, so `TotalPaid = 0`, `AmountDue = $239.68`, and `PaymentStatusLabel` reads *Unpaid* — even though a payment row exists.", "**Promote a customer status into a blocker.** Flip `Statuses.IsBlocking` for *Processing* to TRUE; every customer in that status re-derives `StatusIsBlocking` and `IsStopped` on the next read — no application code touched.", "**Override a stubborn calculation.** Add a row to `ERBCustomizations` with `CustomizationType=FunctionOverride` and Postgres SQL — the next build wires that function in place of the generic transpiled one. See `calc_customers_has_multi_payment_orders` for the canonical example.", "**Read the same balance two ways.** Ask *\"what does Caroline still owe?\"* in the Postgres view (`SELECT amount_due FROM vw_orders WHERE order_id='caroline-1045'`) and again via the rulebook DAG — same zero, no glue code."]

data.Name: journal_seed
data.StringValue: Seven orders on the books, eight payments cleared or pending. Caroline's first card declined and cleared on the second try; Carla's damaged shipment was refunded in full. The DAG keeps the totals honest.

data.Name: CMCC_Summary
data.StringValue: Airtable export with schema-first type mapping: Schemas, Data, Relationships (FK links), Lookups (INDEX/MATCH), Aggregations (SUMIFS/COUNTIFS/Rollups), and Calculated fields (formulas) in Excel dialect. Field types are determined from Airtable's schema metadata FIRST (no coercion), with intelligent fallback to formula/data analysis only when schema is unavailable.

data.Name: conversion_metadata
data.JsonValue: {"source_base_id": "appKLygCIXweUUKtM", "table_count": 8, "tool_version": "2.0.0", "field_type_mapping": "checkbox→boolean, number→number/integer, multipleRecordLinks→relationship, multipleLookupValues→lookup, rollup→aggregation, count→aggregation, formula→calculated", "export_mode": "schema_first_type_mapping", "type_inference": {"enabled": true, "priority": "airtable_metadata (NO COERCION) → formula_analysis → reference_resolution → data_analysis (fallback only)", "airtable_type_mapping": "checkbox→boolean, singleLineText→string, multilineText→string, number→number/integer, datetime→datetime, singleSelect→string, email→string, url→string, phoneNumber→string", "data_coercion_hierarchy": "Only used as fallback when Airtable schema unavailable: datetime → number → integer → boolean → base64 → json → string", "nullable_support": true, "error_value_handling": "#NUM!, #ERROR!, #N/A, #REF!, #DIV/0!, #VALUE!, #NAME? are treated as NULL"}}
